# Production deployment guide

This guide is opinionated. It tells you the dials that matter when
moving signet from `signet serve --dev` to a real production rollout
and explains why each one is here. Follow it top-to-bottom and you
end up with a deployment that is hardened against the failure modes
the threat model in [`security.md`](security.md) calls out.

## TL;DR checklist

If you only read one section, read this. The rest is the *why*.

* Set `SIGNET_HMAC_SECRET` to a 32-byte hex value generated by
  `openssl rand -hex 32`. **Do not** rely on `--allow-ephemeral-key`
  in production: a process restart wipes the verification root.
* Set `SIGNET_AUDIT_LOG_PATH` to a path on durable storage.
* Run with `--workers N>1` only when paired with the
  `FileLockingJsonlBackend` and `HmacChain(cache_prev=False)`. See
  [Multi-process workers](#multi-process-workers) below.
* Keep `--strict-error-redaction` (the default) on. The verbose body
  shape leaks check names and rule labels, which is operational
  intelligence.
* Put real authentication in front of signet. Caller-asserted owner
  headers are attribution, not authentication. mTLS or an OIDC
  proxy at the edge: see [`integrations/auth.md`](integrations/auth.md).
* Configure an external anchor backend
  (`Rfc3161Anchor` against FreeTSA is the lowest-friction option) so
  audit-chain timestamps are independently verifiable.
* Pick the right HTTP probe: `/healthz` for liveness, `/readyz`
  for readiness. `/readyz` probes the upstream and 503s when it is
  unreachable; pair it with k8s readiness so traffic sheds without
  killing the pod.

The remaining sections expand each item.

## TLS and the trust boundary

signet is deliberately not a TLS terminator. Run a reverse proxy
(nginx, caddy, an ALB, an envoy sidecar) that:

1. Terminates TLS for inbound clients. signet serves cleartext HTTP
   on `127.0.0.1` (the default `--host`) and trusts that nobody else
   is on that interface.
2. Strips client-supplied owner headers (`X-Commit-Owner`, `X-Agent-Id`,
   `X-Policy-Name`, `X-Policy-Version`) and rewrites them based on the
   authenticated identity. Otherwise any client that can reach signet
   can claim to be anyone, because signet does not authenticate the
   headers themselves — see the warning in
   [`checks/owner_resolution.md`](checks/owner_resolution.md).
3. Optionally enforces mTLS. The
   [`integrations/auth.md`](integrations/auth.md) doc has working
   nginx, FastAPI middleware, and oauth2-proxy recipes for this.

Binding signet directly to `0.0.0.0` is supported for niche
deployments (single-tenant, network-isolated), but the default is
loopback for a reason. Use `--host 0.0.0.0` only after working
through the trust-model section in `architecture.md`.

## CSRF posture

For server-to-server callers (the default agent and tool-router shape),
CSRF is not a concern: there is no browser, no cookie, no implicit
authority. Owner attribution comes from explicit headers your edge
proxy injects.

If you mount signet behind a browser-fronted UI (chat product, admin
console) on the *same* origin as authenticated session cookies, treat
it like any cookie-authenticated API:

* Require a CSRF token on every state-changing request.
* Set `SameSite=Strict` on the auth cookie.
* Set `cors_allow_credentials=False` unless you fully understand the
  consequences. The default is `False`.
* Do **not** use `cors_allowed_origins=("*",)` with credentials —
  the browser and the spec will refuse the combination, but signet
  will accept the misconfiguration at startup.

## Worker count and `cache_prev`

The simplest production deployment is a single-worker process. It
scales further than people expect: signet's per-request work is
microseconds, the upstream LLM is the bottleneck.

When you do scale to multiple uvicorn workers (`--workers N>1`):

* Use `FileLockingJsonlBackend` instead of the default `JsonlBackend`.
  It serializes appends with `fcntl` (POSIX) / `msvcrt` (Windows) so
  two workers cannot fork the chain.
* Pass `cache_prev=False` to `HmacChain`. Each append re-reads the
  chain head under the cross-process lock; without this, a worker's
  cached `prev_hmac` will race ahead of a sibling worker's writes
  and the next append will dangle.

```python
from pathlib import Path

from signet.audit.backend import FileLockingJsonlBackend
from signet.audit.chain import HmacChain
from signet.audit.keyring import Key, KeyRing

ring = KeyRing(active=Key(key_id="prod-2026-q2", secret=...))
chain = HmacChain(
    backend=FileLockingJsonlBackend(Path("/var/log/signet/audit.jsonl")),
    keyring=ring,
    cache_prev=False,  # required when running uvicorn --workers > 1
)
```

If you forget either piece, the verifier catches it post-hoc — but
the failure mode is "we cannot prove the audit chain wasn't tampered
with after the fact", which defeats half the point of running signet.

## Anchor-backend selection

The audit chain's HMAC is tamper-evident *if* you trust the secret.
External anchoring binds each entry to an independent witness so
verification doesn't depend on the secret remaining secret.

Pick by threat model:

| Backend | Threat covered | Cost | Friction |
|---|---|---|---|
| `NoopAnchor` (default) | None — chain is HMAC-only | Free | None |
| `Rfc3161Anchor(url=FreeTSA)` | "Did this entry exist by time T?" against any third party who trusts FreeTSA | Free | One outbound HTTP call per append |
| `Rfc3161Anchor(url=your-internal-TSA)` | Same, against an authority you control | Cost of your TSA | One outbound call |
| Custom `AnchorBackend` (Sigstore Rekor, internal transparency log) | Public log inclusion proof | Varies | Implement the protocol |

For a first production deployment, `Rfc3161Anchor` against FreeTSA
is the lowest-friction option that gives you something. Move to a
TSA you control once compliance asks for it.

## HSM / KMS integration

The HMAC secret in `KeyRing` is treated as cleartext bytes by signet.
For production, the secret should never leave a hardware security
module: signet should call out to KMS to compute the per-entry HMAC.

This is plugin territory in v0.1.x. The audit-chain interface that
matters is `HmacChain._read_prev_hmac()` (read) and the inline
`hmac.new(...)` call inside `append()` (write). Wrap your KMS in an
implementation that satisfies the same contract and pass it via a
custom subclass.

This is one of the genuine "you'll want signet support for this"
items — see [the README's "When you need more than the OSS" section](https://github.com/jeranaias/signet#when-you-need-more-than-the-oss)
if you'd rather have it engineered.

## Probe wiring

| Endpoint | Use for | Returns |
|---|---|---|
| `/health` (alias `/healthz`) | k8s liveness | 200 with `{status, version, uptime_seconds, audit_chain_head_hmac, pipeline_check_count}`. Always 200 if the process is alive. |
| `/readyz` | k8s readiness | 200 if upstream answered within 1s; 503 with `{status, reason, upstream}` otherwise. |
| `/version` | build-time identification | `{version, service}`. |
| `/metrics` | Prometheus scrape | Prometheus exposition format. See the metrics module for the counter set. |

Wire `livenessProbe` to `/healthz` and `readinessProbe` to `/readyz`.
Don't point both probes at `/health` — a flaky upstream will then
restart the pod instead of just shedding traffic.

## Logging

`signet serve --log-format json` routes the stdlib `logging` calls
through structlog and emits one JSON object per line on stderr. Wire
that to your aggregator (Loki / Datadog / ELK / OpenTelemetry
collector) and you get structured logs without rewriting any
modules.

Audit logs are *not* the same thing as application logs. The audit
chain is the security record (`audit.jsonl`), the application log
is the operational stream (request volumes, upstream latencies,
errors). Don't conflate them; they have different retention and
different compliance posture.

## Strict-error redaction

Default in v0.1.5. Refusal bodies surface only:

```json
{"error": "refused", "correlation_id": "8f7e2c1a-..."}
```

The check name, the firing rule, the severity — all stay in the
audit row, surfaced via `correlation_id`. Operators who need that
detail tail the audit log; attackers do not. Disable
(`--no-strict-error-redaction`) only for first-time integration
debugging; the default is what production should run.

## What signet does not handle

* TLS termination — see [TLS and the trust boundary](#tls-and-the-trust-boundary).
* Caller authentication — owner headers are claims, not proofs. See
  [`integrations/auth.md`](integrations/auth.md).
* Secret rotation — `KeyRing.rotate()` exists but the operational
  flow (when, how often, with what key-management substrate) is your
  call.
* Long-term audit retention — JSONL on disk is the wire format; how
  you back it up, replicate it across regions, or stream it into a
  WORM-compliant store is your call.
